sentence: The patient reports that his acne is doing better, excellent, and he has been using the benzoyl peroxide wash, antibiotic lotion, tretinoin cream, and sunscreen moisture stuff as directed.
He has not experienced any redness, peeling, dryness, or irritation with the use of these medications.
He has been washing his face with the 4-5% benzoyl peroxide wash, applying the tretinoin cream at night, and using the sunscreen moisture stuff twice daily.

He mentions that washing his chest and back with a higher strength benzoyl peroxide wash is not a priority as it has not bothered him much, but may be something he is more interested in pursuing as he moves into the summer.
He feels that the acne on his face is improving.
Physical exam: There are scattered inflammatory papules and pustules across the upper back and posterior shoulders, with less than five inflammatory papules on the forehead, cheeks, and chin.
Assessment: The patient's acne vulgaris is improved and well-controlled on the face but has a mild flare on the back.
Problem list: Acne vulgaris; Acne vulgaris (back) status: active; Acne vulgaris (face) status: active.

I recommended continuing the current treatment plan for the face, which includes the benzoyl peroxide wash, antibiotic lotion, and tretinoin cream, and initiating treatment with Panoxyl 10% foaming wash on the back in the shower daily.
I also prescribed Panoxyl 10% to the patient, with instructions to use it daily.
I sent the patient home with samples and coupons for the Panoxyl 10% and refilled the patient's prescription for clindamycin with 11 refills and tretinoin cream with 3 refills.
I instructed the patient to return in one year for a follow-up appointment to review his treatment plan and refill his prescriptions as needed.
